commentBody:
Never heard of a RED WINE SUPERNOVA before.

(But I'm psyched! The Jacobian Conjecture has just been settled — in the negative.)

I am shocked red wine super nova wasn’t the seed entry.  Last summer? You couldn’t go anywhere without hearing a Chappell roan song. 

Extra points for the fun themer spanners.  

Nice Tuesday. 

Thanks Kenneth.

I've been doing NYT puzzles since I was 11 and I'm 63. I got personal records both yesterday and today. Granted, I don't often do Mondays or Tuesdays anymore, and I know the puzzles have eased up in general over time, but I thought I'd brag anyway.

Nagasaki crossing with Bada Bing Bada Boom...yikes, talk about triggering for some folk! I'll be curious if the constructor gets any flak for that one. Hello Daddy, Hello Mom: I'm your ch-ch-ch-cherry bomb! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_EBvXpjudf8&list=RD_EBvXpjudf8&start_radio=1
I don’t usually try to scoop Mike, but I have to today—this puzzle just made me so emotional, I got a little big bang teary.
Being more familiar with hot peppers than Cuban dancing, I had 8D as HABANERO. This made 34A GO OUT WITH A BONG. Wrong maybe but I hope to see this in a future puzzle.
I still never remember the difference between song/record/album/single. I kept trying to fit Pink Pony Club or Midwest Princess in there at first

Heh. Describing Carmen as a comic opera is a choice!  I did some Wikipedia diving: While Carmen is written in the opéra comique style (spoken dialogue as well as songs), do not mistake it for an opera that is comic.

(What I did not need Wikipedia for is the knowledge that the opera has themes of jealousy and possessiveness and lust, and the ending is distinctly not happy)

(But I love the music, especially "L'amour est un oiseau rebelle" (the habanera) and the Toreador theme)
Red Wine Supernova is not a record, it's a single off of the record, you should fix that error.

Well, I'll leave you no longer in suspense...

I did come back from my blue star Monday to get a gold star Tuesday.  

Thank you.  Thank you very much.  You're way too kind.  Your applause is music to my ears.

Pretty impressive grid for a Tuesday, and some fun theme material.

Andrzej, you might appreciate this—BADABINGBADABOOM reminds me of the “Badabing, Badabang” episode from Star Trek Deep Space Nine, and brings back fond memories of James Darren’s role as the holodeck crooner Vic Fontaine. One of Star Trek’s most engaging holodeck characters, in my opinion.
I have never seen “hep” before, is that a term that is used??
Surprised a song with those lyrics would be a hot one. Will we ever stop with lyrics that are demeaning to women?

In case BOOM, BLAST, BANG, and BURST are triggering in a negative way to anyone, here's a song to help you reframe: Bob Schneider with "The World Exploded Into Love" – https://youtu.be/S9udALiWXbM?is=aMyBlY0TLXjHihmf As for me, I thought the puzzle was creative and fun. easy breezy and over too soon. Thank you, Mr. Cortes! (For yet another musical interlude, here's Roxy Music with OH YEAH – https://youtu.be/EfCAHjm9ox0?is=1OPMr2cg7wrnaEqg)

Potential spoiler alert for the end of Kurt Vonnegut's "Cat's Cradle".

Spoiler below...

May I add my favorite to the lists of elegiac sounds.  Kurt Vonnegut's "Grand Ah-whoom" in the breathtaking "Cat's Cradle".

From the master himself:

"There was a sound like that of the gentle closing of a portal as big as the sky, the great door of heaven being closed softly. It was a grand AH-WHOOM."

I can hear it in my head.  I think everyone can hear it in their heads, too.

Sam, you’re not alone! I too didn’t know that a record could mean a single song. I was very curious how Rise and Fall of a Midwest Princess was going to fit into 16 spaces and how it related to BANGs.
Please bring back the theme and tricky clues header at the top of the wordplay. I hate accidentally getting theme spoilers when I just need help with a tricky clue
TRIVET?

I thought this was a really good Tuesday! Was pleasantly stumped by the horizontal clues but moved  quickly after that. Appreciated a few of the longer entries. My solve time was the same but the process felt better than usual.

I thought this was nice for a Tuesday--a little stiffer than the usual five-finger exercises. I want to check out REDWINESUPERNOVA simply for the name.

Also, it gives me the opportunity to quote Dorothy Parker on Ayn Rand: “Atlas Shrugged is not a novel to be tossed aside lightly. It should be thrown with great force.”

IMNSHO, Rand was a deeply damaged person, unworthy to be called either a philosopher or novelist, who translated her trauma into one of the most irrational, hateful and self-centered systems of belief ever devised. I wish she would disappear from crossword puzzles and college curricula.

YMMV, as always.
"The Rise And Fall of a Midwestern Princess" is the name of Chappell Roan's 2023 record! "Red Wine Supernova" is a single from it!

My principal piano mentor at The University of Michigan, Marian Owen, a native of Cuba, insisted that a real Habaňera went slower than the familiar Carmen aria. She scorned the Carmen Habañera as “nothing but a cheap Spanish tango.”

As your resident alphadoppeltotter, a role I’ve inexplicably taken in the past eight years, it is my duty to inform you that this puzzle has an unusually low number of double letters, at four, where unusual is any number less than five. This is the fourth time this year that this has happened. 

I remain your humble servant, ever on the alert.

Well, the Libra in me achieved ultimate balance from this puzzle.

On one hand, the fireworks, ka-pow, and kablooie of the theme kindled buoyancy and spark.  Here was a puzzle that truly had pop.  It brought me alive, got me buzzing.

On the other, as I looked over the completed grid, with its remarkably junk-free answer set, I went to a zen place of calm, of serenity.  This doesn’t happen very often; it takes a special puzzle to trigger it.  I’m never looking for it; it just happens.

Vibrant and chill, now that’s a rare and wonderful state of mind to be in, and that’s how I left the box today.  That is a gift, Kenneth.  Thank you for making this!

When my Acer laptop is in sleep mode, if I try to wake it, it hisses at me. Is this just a setting I can change, or should I be worried?

OGRE OHGROWUP is my crossword earworm for the day.
And my mantra.

A delightful puzzle, with an appropriate mix of gimmes and head-scratchers for a Tuesday. I was surprised, though, when I got the star and saw my time was 40% under my average. I can’t think of BADDA-BING-BADDA-BOOM without thinking of the film Fifth Element. https://youtu.be/j8WLYzA0lCs?is=sqdlEuyddnYN6shF
I initially read 5A as "Gnome component," and I confidently entered HAT. Oops. Let's get the coffee brewing!

Banging great puzzle today!  Bringing some energy on a Tuesday.

So much of life is timing, enough said about that.

And thanks for the Pink Pony Club earworm now on repeat in my brain.

“Sport-ute”?

I get the portmanteau-ish thing here, but here in the land of ATVs and many an SUV, I’ve never heard anyone say this. 

Just wondering if it’s a regional thing (and if so, where) or new (to me) crosswordese or?…
Towards the end I got caught up by reading the clue for HEINIE as [Backslide].  Nice one.  Thanks.

Yesterday, in response to the difference between "less" and "fewer," I posted a link to Weird Al's parody, "Word Crimes." I'll repost that link today, as there is a reference to today's puzzle, at 2:42--don't blink or you'll miss it!: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8Gv0H-vPoDc&list=RD8Gv0H-vPoDc&start_radio=1

The heat is getting to me:  I just left a Comment about today's puzzle on yesterday's WordPlay.  Oops.  

Now:

I was up early, did the puzzle, but had to go out to water the languishing plants after our 108 degree heat index that has everything drooping...plants, humans, and even the lightposts. 

So, here I am again, beached on the prongs of a trident (candy, actress, REDWINESUPERNOVA, Sport-UTE,oops-too-many.) I got them all, but all I really wanted was for the puzzle to be over so I could go get more ice water.

 Fave entry was HABAÑERA... My sister and I used to sing along with Risé Stevens while we did the dishes. Ah, Carmen.....

Tried and failed to solve this one using just the horizontal clues. Had the top half in place---including the revealer---and odds and ends in the bottom half. Finally gave up and resorted to verticals, which allowed me to finish the puzzle easily enough.

Just couldn't figure out the last two thematic entries. "Taffy" and STARBURST simply didn't connect in my head, and the Chappell Roan record was a total unknown.

Furthermore, with endings like BOOM, BLAST, BANG and BURST, REDWINESUPERNOVA doesn't even seem thematic to me. And SUPERNOVA basically duplicates STARBURST.

To me, this puzzle is thematically busted, and I would have sent it back for reworking. And, much more horrifying, "Which person are you referring to?" calls for WHOM, not WHO.     ;-)
Fun Tuesday!  Thank you, Mr. Cortes.

"Starbursts" are, to me, a type of hard candy. They are white disks, maybe 3/4" in diameter and 1/4" thick, with rounded edges and a bright red starburst  design radiating from the center. They are popular with a lot of factory workers who are forbidden to eat while working. They don't require chewing, and you can still speak with one tucked away in a cheek, so they are nearly undetectable.

I never used them, because I knew the consequences of having essentially a hard lump of sugar resting against your teeth for hours: decay, unhealthy gums, and insulin resistance. But I appreciated the small act of rebellion.

Clever theme! 

I was Today’s years old when I learned that Starbursts are indeed a form of taffy… 😂 

BADA BING BADA BOOM!

Rating:
9/10

The fact that “record” (not recording) can refer to one song, and not the entire album is proof that the English language and the world is going down the dumpster, or maybe that I’m just a grumpy boomer (and a grandfather as of yesterday).
Coincidentally, when I looked for a version of HEPcat's "Dance Wid' Me" to post, this version from the SUPERNOVA Ska Festival came up. https://youtu.be/okYIKKHBrE8?si=c5qqtodnoz8lAgZW

I came to the column expecting a picture of a BODEGA cat. Left disappointed.

Wow, am I out  of the loop - I didn't  even know Chappell Roan was a female singer. But at least I don't own any "records." I do know all the words to Champagne SUPERNOVA.

Proud Papa warning. Here's the video for REDWINESUPERNOVA, edited by...my daughter Raven! https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=VS6ixn2berk&list=RDVS6ixn2berk&start_radio=1&pp=ygUScmVkIHdpbmUgc3VwZXJub3ZhoAcB

Most of the puzzle was fun and quick today, but I have nitpicks about the SE corner.  Having the big clue RED WINE SUPERNOVA for people not familiar with the artist was tough to guess when it crossed HEP which really feels like it should be HIP.

Then also crossing TRIVET which I’ve never h heard of, a Mexican geography clue and TEASE which I don’t understand as an answer to Backcomb, all next to each other.
The best use of SKINT is, I think, by Sidney Poitier in To Sir With Love.

Late addition - thought it was interesting that one of the non theme answers was NAGASAKI, since it could have fit in with the theme, in a different way.

.....
Um Actually, WoW is not an RPG but a MMORPG. If you are going to use our games and our terms use them correctly.

Re Sam's comment on "record" in the column, I'm old enough to remember when you could buy a record with a single song on it.* Don't know if anyone still makes 45s anymore, but I've heard vinyl albums have had a resurgence so maybe? (There were/are also CD singles, but I've never bought one.) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_(music) * Well, actually two, as there is/was (usually) one song on each side.
Did it, didn't like it. A number of questionable clues. Taffy?
I finished the puzzle and had to nitpick to find my error. I was confusing the Cuban dance style with the HABANERo pepper, but then I realized you can't really GO OUT WITH A BoNG. Or maybe you can...

poem made from words in today’s puzzle


a/ someone found a haw
under the sink, a clean haw 
with colorful tips 
and d/ 21 pips!
made its home, it has
to mature 
in some corner
in standard hot/cold conditions
it has a long a/ sleep season
yeah

One more puzzle find - a Sunday from May 30, 1971 by Keith Blake with the title "Extra Values." Some theme answers: NICELYNICELY ABSALOMABSALOM MERRILYMERRILY JOHNJOHN TEMPERTEMPER And then a pair of answers, the second referencing the first as a clue: MILESTOGOBEFOREISLEEP MILESTOGOBEFOREISLEEP Don't recall another one quite like that. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=5/30/1971&h=8d ....
Note to Sam:Carmen as a comic opera? Too many people die.

Fun Tuesday puzzle. I didn't know the Chappell Roan record but got it from crosses. Like many others, apparently, I never thought of Starburst candy as a type of toffee either, but wasn't bothered by that. Thanks for the entertainment, Kenneth Cortez.

I must have  missed the history lesson that HEP meant cool. 
What "old school" era was that?
Good puzzle 🧩 but what is Sport-UTE?
The same people complaining about not knowing REDWINESUPERNOVA are the same ones who have something to say when the youth doesn’t know songs from the 60s and 70s.. oh well, shrug
Assay

Wait, BODEGA is in fact just a corner store?? I've been told by New Yorkers for years that it is something so holy and sacred that us simple folk in the middle of the country can't even comprehend a corner store within walking distance that has groceries and a deli, something that exists all over the country.
I rarely do Tuesday puzzles but had a go at this one.  And it was great!  Enough bite to tax the brain cells a bit, some clever entries and clueing -- well done!

@Dan That's interesting ... counter-example in dimension three found by Claude Fable 5, Anthropic's LLM. So only the general case in dimension two remains. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacobian_conjecture

@Weak
I  didn't notice the extra column until I read, well, the column.

@john ezra

Too soon?
@Steve L And always will be

@john ezra You're really covering all the bases with "This might be triggering to some folk" and "Here's a link to a 50-year-old song with 'bomb' in the title!"

And if we're being technical, wouldn't crossing Nagasaki with "Go out with a bang" be far worse?

@Steve L

So we can start make jokes about concentration camps. Right?  
Timeframe matches up. 

Too soon?

@Matt, I'm not sure I'm covering any bases at all, but boom, blast, bang -- the point is maybe it was a wise choice to include Nagasaki as an echo of the theme, or maybe it was an ill-considered decision to have a potentially controversial name of a city associated with the Bomb. I don't get triggered by anything myself (well, images and voices of certain people will set me off, but not puzzle entries) so I don't have a problem with it, but I do wonder how the editorial process worked on including Nagasaki, and find it impossible to guess their thought processes. 

As for Cherry Bomb, it's my favorite song about bombs, so I thought I'd pop in a link to it!

@Weak

Did you miss Hogan’s Heroes?  In the 1960s?

@john ezra

So long, Mom.
I'm off to drop the bomb,
So don't wait up for me.
But though I may roam,
I'll come back to my home,
Although it may be
A pile of debris.

@Steve L
Hogan's Heroes was set in a POW camp. I could be wrong, because it's been a long time since I watched it and it might have gone over my head as a kid, but I don't recall anything set at a concentration camp.

@Vaer

Hoe about “Springtime for H!tler” from the movie of the same era?  (The original “Producers.”)

@Vaer

Third attempt. 

What about the song from the Producers, circa 1967, with the song about springtime for that German guy?

Seems the National Socialist regime was satirized pretty soon after the fact.

@Steve L

Whut?  
You are trying to compare a comedy show based at a POW camp that made fun of Germans and made them look like buffoons

To

People being burned alive by an atomic bomb. 

Maybe I’m mistaken.  Did hogan’s heroes make fun of Jews suffering at a concentration camp.  I must’ve missed those episodes   

Borrowing from sergeant Schultz. 
“ you know nothing!”

@Steve L
The Producers, set in NYC, and that song make fun of that guy and his minions not the camps.

@Weak

No, I’m comparing it to the implication that the answer NAGASAKI belongs in a puzzle whose theme is a group of innocuous words and phrases that end in explosive words.

@SBK in TO
Dresden makes me think of Slaughterhouse Five, which covers both bases.  The teapot and the non-linear storytelling broke my heart and exploded my prepubescent head.
@SP -- THAT HURT!  In a good way...
@Mark   Too funny. Light ’er up…
@Mark That was my last correction before the star!

@Tom C.


Casual. Is the star of the show. The lyrics are intense. But I love how raw it is.

@Charles Peterson
I'd posit that they are still physical objects of various sizes. There continue to be pressings of new material in LP, EP, and 45 (7") versions. I don't think anyone is still printing 78s or 16s.  Remember 16s? I think they mostly came where you had to rip them out of a magazine.

@Isabeau Welp, my head did explode a little when I read that description. Perhaps Sam was channeling the Gilligan's Island musical adaption of Hamlet? https://youtu.be/Kz3yL9Jg8bo?si=S-U9FPnXMD42Lmb0 https://youtu.be/12sVIlQ6bkQ?si=xV-ix9gs82wx8hiC
@Vaer Every once in a while, I will get the Gilligan’s Island Hamlet stuck in my head. Not sure why, but it happens, and it always makes me smile. Thank you for this.

@Vaer

I almost wrote about that and thought "No, no one is as nerdy as me and would understand that Gilligan's Island reference."
@Vaer @Isabeau This is what I get for having a bilingual brain 🤦‍♀️

@Sam Corbin
At least you know we read the column. :)

@Francis,
Totally would have understood that Gilligan’s Island reference. In fact, went out to YouTube to show my 30-year old daughter that episode as recently as a few weeks ago! (And it may have been spurred by something that came up in whatever NYT Xword we did that day!)

@Vaer
With Phil Silvers in the role of theater impresario Harold Hecuba!  Ah, those were the days, eh?

@Vaer
partial head explosion describes my experience pretty well. I came to the comments ready to comment, but @Isabeau elegantly beat me to it.

@Sam Corbin
I'm jealous of your bilingual brain, and amused at how it served you today. And glad you circled back.

@Kyle

A single is a record, and an album is a record.

@Kyle
Is the confusion because people are too young to remember singles (45 RPMs) being called records? Back in my day, we called singles "records" and the collection was a "record album."

@Puzzled I think it’s also that no one buys physicals anymore. If they went to a streaming app, they’d see the singles and albums there, which are both records.  Also since the song is on a studio album, everyone probably assumes it’s simply a song on the album.

@Thatbroad

The Grammys award Record of the Year to a single or album track. Albums qualify for Album of the Year, but not Record of the Year.

@Puzzled

Yeah, and the record album was kind like a photo album. A book with paper sleeves that each held a heavy, brittle 45.

@Joe

I think you mean a heavy, brittle 33 1/3.

@Don H

Nope.  Those sets that gave rise to the term “album” were the brittle 78s. 45s were never sold in album form with any regularity, and sets of multiple LPs (33s) were called “double albums,” “triple albums,” and “boxed sets.”  But even single LPs, by far the most common LP packaging, were called “albums,” as they replaced the albums of 78s in the 50s.
@Puzzled That's interesting. I got stumped on that answer because I think of singles as singles and albums as records. This was a very slow Tuesday for me

@Puzzled I have never in my 66 years heard anyone call an album a "record album". But we did call them 'records' and still do. It's only the Grammys that insist that a 'record' is a single. Records come in various sizes. e.g.: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Rws_7mLTqj8&list=RDRws_7mLTqj8&start_radio=1&pp=ygUMQmlnIHRlbiBpbmNooAcB

@Charles Peterson

I'm only 12 years older than you, but I've always called it a record album.  At our house when I was a kid we had albums that were several 45's in paper sleeves.  Then when 78's came out they could put all those songs on one record!  But it was still called an album, because it was a selection of songs.

@Francis

I'm happy to provide the visuals for you:

👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻
👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻

👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻👏🏻

@Bill

I appreciate the post!

Towards the end of last week some thunderstorms came through improving the air quality to merely "Very unhealthy".

So I took the opportunity to drive through to the relatively better airs of the Twin Cities, where I am now.

I think at this point parts of Wisconsin and Michigan have it worse than the Minnesota North Shore.

@Mean Old Lady
Yes  it's Canute J. Trump at work.

@SP

Indeed, he was a great character, with surprising depth. I recently rewatched DS9 and I found it much better than what I remembered from the 1990s. Apparently it's a show you appreciate more as an adult than a teenager. Maybe because human (and non-human) interest seems to be at the forefront, rather than straight up adventure?

As for the character, when I first the the episode I may have looked at the screen with a dog-like tilt of the head: is that the guy from "The Guns of Navarone"?! And it was!

Did you really remember the title of the episode? I recall some ST episode titles 
("The Menagerie" of the original series, "All Good Things..." of TNG), but definitely a huge minority of them.

@SP

I was into the original Star Trek series--when I was studying for my qualifying exams in grad school I'd take a break every day to watch reruns.

As I read, I tried to imagine Mr. Spock explaining it to me with his distinctive enunciation.

But I never got past the Second Generation.  

It sounds like Deep Space Nine would be a good place to pick back up?
Also meant to include @Andrzej on that reply.

@Francis

The Next Generation, then DS9 and Voyager. Another sequence makes little sense.

@Andrzej
Yes I did remember the title, nerd that I am. 
Like you I didn’t appreciate Deep Space Nine as much  the first time around (I wasn’t a teenager, but still) but in re-viewing I also thought it was amazing, and one of the more mature and well-developed, well acted, Star Trek series. 
There’s a great documentary on it, one of the best documentaries on the making of a TV show I can remember, you might want to check it out:

What We Left Behind: Looking Back at Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.

@Francis
Yes I would highly recommend checking it out. As with most Star Trek shows, the first season or two can be a bit uneven, so you have to stick with it.

@Francis
Deep Space Nine (DS9) is the best of the series, in my opinion, especially after the first couple seasons. It differs from the earlier series by having multi-episode, season-long story arcs, and a darker tone. It has two of the most interesting villains, or quasi-villains, Gul Dukat and Garak. And, for comic relief, it has the Ferengi--the interactions between my Partner and my Brother-in-Law often reminds me of Quark and Rom.

@Bill

However, if Francis last watched the original series, I think TNG is a necessary introduction to the world of Star Trek of the 1980s and 1990s. DS9 is a connoisseur's spin-off of TNG, really, and without the base material it may fall a bit flat, no?

@SP

I'm not really into meta-content, such as documentaries about shows, but one about ST might be interesting. Thanks.

@Andrzej

This one was unusually entertaining and insightful, and was interspersed with the writers brainstorming how they might have continued the series which was exceedingly interesting.

@SP

It’s hard keeping up with the Cardassians.

@SP

Underappreciated by whom? I have always found the character interesting, and the actor's performance was above the ST average, too.

@SP

I must say I wasn't particularly fond of the religious twist things took by the end of the show... It might be interesting to hear whether alternatives had been considered.

@SP

Meh, the bots ate my innocuous reply...
@SP - I too thought of that episode when filling in that answer, and had bang at the end instead of boom for awhile as a result. Deep Space Nine was my favorite Star Trek series.

@Andrzej
I'm surprised you don't know more about baseball if you're a DS9 fan.  ;)

I'm a fan of DS9 and not much of a fan of baseball, but DS9 gave me a different way of looking at it. A B'way play, TAKE ME OUT, did the same thing.

FWIW I've been enjoying Strange New Worlds. Hit it.

@Matt  

yeah, those cats got hip.
@Cathy King Well sure, I guess a song written by a woman can be demeaning to women. But I did not see that in this song.

@Cathy King Just because a song is about a sexual experience with a woman doesn’t mean that it’s demeaning to that woman.  You don’t have to like the song, but there is no disrespectful language in it.

@Francis
Wonderful

@Francis
The sound of Ice-9!

@Francis
everytime I see a stack of cannonballs....
@Elena Glad I’m not alone, and at least we both learned something new!

@Elena
What?

We have some Old 78's that don't even have anything on the flip side.  And what about 45's?  
Did I read/understand this comment correctly?

@Mean Old Lady You did read it right! I grew up in the Spotify era of music. I never had any vinyls, and I assumed they were all full albums because that’s the only kind of vinyl I’ve ever seen a modern artist sell. You learn something new every day!

@Elena
Hey, we could play you a Caruso-Gallicurchi duet that would knock your socks off!  Tito Schipa!  Schumann-Heink!

@Elena
gah.

"vinyls"

Nothing personal but gah.
@Elena This is probably a sign of the irrelevance of the Grammys, considering their "Record of the Year" usually goes to a single song.

@Sam Corbin 
And thank you for the definition of "skint." It lead me to one of my favorite words: impecunious!

@Kristen
?
I see the headings.
Did there used to be direct links or simething?
Yes! There used to be direct links, and I agree, I don’t like spoiling the theme when I just need help with one or two clues to break into a puzzle

@Andrzej 
Yeah. Once I had enough crossings I realized I had heard of it before, but pretty obscure for a Tuesday. And while we're at it: Sport-UTE? I got it with crossings, but had absolutely no idea what it was until I looked it up.

@Andrzej
A stumpy three legged stand, usually made of metal, that you place hot pans on to save damaging a kitchen worktop. Possibly a little old fashioned now as worktops have become much more robust. Unless you foolishly go for wood as a friend of mine did. Scorch marks and indelible stains from cumin, chilli etc. mark her cooking journey. No, she didn’t use a TRIVET.

@Matt

I looked it up. We simply call it "podkładka", literally "the thing you place under something" - and that applies to both a coaster and what apparently you know as a trivet. I have either never heard or seen the English name, or have not retained it. I'm the one who does 95% of the cooking in our household, but I don't use trivets. I do my plating in the kitchen, where stone countertops require no protection from the heat of the pots and pans 🤷🏽

@Helen Wright

Stone countertops FTW. Ours are of granite, I think - they came with the condo when we bought it, so I can't be sure, but that's what they look like. They have proved to be practically indestructible over the years.

@Andrzej
Yep, we’re the same. Basically indestructible.

@Andrzej

TRIVET is used on the regs in my house.

 Our son made one in shop class
and it’s displayed on the table.

(And we have at least five others.)

My husband once called it a “trestle”
so he finds it funny

every…single…time…

to refer to our trivets as trestles.

@Andrzej

Also, our dining room table
is as delicate as it is beautiful,

As a two time ready of "Atlas Shrugged" I enthusiastically agreed.

Both times when I read it (I was about 16, then about 20) I was initially really impressed.  Then every time I thought about it, another chunk fell off--another completely contrived plot point or event, a world filled with one-dimensional characters.  And within a couple of years, there was nothing left.

Glad to hear you and Dorothy Parker agree.

@Francis
@PuzzleDog

I can't ever recall being more annoyed at a recommender and enraged by a book than when I read "Atlas Shrugged."

I found it odious in every way, from tortuous style to execrable worldview to character two-dimensionality to flaccid pacing.

And her "philosophic" works are poorly reasoned and entirely tendentious.

I always look for an opportunity to call her out as among the most untalented famous writers/thinkers since the Enlightenment.

Thank you both.

I read Atlas Shrugged and The Fountainhead as a very young teenager. @Times Rita

Yes, I had my "admiring John Galt" phase.  

It didn't last very long, and I'm very embarrassed about it.

It's one thing to exalt people with enormous imaginations and intellect.  It's another thing to have nothing but complete hatred and disdain for anyone who isn't in those hallowed halls.

I've been lucky enough to meet several Nobel prize winners, mostly in physics and chemistry.  And none of them, not a single one, was anywhere near as egotistical and narcissistic as Rand.

People who are truly great don't have to spend their lives telling everyone how great they are.
